questions.description: Jack wants to sell his car, so on Monday he puts an advertisement up on… Advise Jack.
questions.text: Jack wants to sell his car, so on Monday he puts an advertisement up on the car selling website, carsforsale.ie, which states: "Private Sale: Renault Paguna, 2013. 5 door, Diesel, 100,000km. In excellent condition. €10,000". That evening he receives an email from Paula, who says "I'm very interested in your car. Would you accept €9,000 for it?" Jack immediately replies, stating "Sorry, I'm only willing to sell for €10,000." On Tuesday he receives an email from Sam, who is willing to pay the full €10,000 for the car. Jack is delighted and immediately replies to confirm the sale. Sam takes the car for a test drive that evening, pays the full €10,000 and takes the car. On Wednesday, Jack receives another email from Paula, which states: "Okay, I'll take it for €10,000." When Jack replies to say he has sold the car to Sam, Paula is furious. She insists that they have a contract and that Jack has to sell her the car. Four weeks after the car has been sold to Sam, Jack receives another email from Sam. In this email, Sam complains about the condition of the car, saying that Jack never told him that the clutch was in need of repairs costing €900. He says that the car is not as advertised and not of merchantable quality, and wants Jack to pay for the repairs. Advise Jack.

questions.description: Fred operates a plant hire business, hiring out bulky machinery for the… Frustrat.
questions.text: Fred operates a plant hire business, hiring out bulky machinery for the construction and demolition of buildings and roads. He leases a site from Maire intending to use it for storing and recycling the machinery and materials. The 5 year lease provides that Fred may "use the premises only for the storage and recycling of machinery and bulk road materials or for such other purposes as may from time to time be approved in writing by the Landlord". After using this site for one month, noise and dust from his operations lead to complaints. The local council serves an enforcement notice on Fred and Maire, ordering them to cease using the site for the recycling of construction and demolition materials on the grounds that this violates existing planning laws. Fred claims that the lease has been frustrated as the order renders illegal the only use of the site which was permitted under the lease and/or the only use of relevance to him. He claims that the property is of no value to him now and that the likelihood of sub-leasing the site is minimal. Advise Fred.

questions.description: He wishes to obtain a loan of €150,000 from his bank to add an extra… Advise Sally.
questions.text: John owns a restaurant. He wishes to obtain a loan of €150,000 from his bank to add an extra dining room and larger kitchen to the restaurant premises. His wife Sally inherited a house in Dublin city from her aunt, and John tells Sally that he needs her to act as a guarantor for the loan and to charge her interest in the house as security for the loan. Sally agrees to this as she is worried about their marriage and is worried John will leave her. When Sally is in the bank, she is asked to sign a form which states that she has received dependent advice from a solicitor, but in fact she has not received any such legal advice. When she queries signing the form, the bank manager states "Oh it's just a formality. We never enforce these things anyway. You can go talk to your solicitor about it if you're worried though." Sally signs, but does not consult her solicitor at any point. By November 2020 John is in serious difficulties. He has failed to pay any of his loan repayments and Sally is worried that the bank will take possession of her house. Advise Sally.

questions.description: Tracey runs a business manufacturing and selling skin care creams made… Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Tracey runs a business manufacturing and selling skin care creams made from ethically produced ingredients in eco-friendly packaging. The packaging for her products is supplied by Potek Packaging Ltd, which specialises in eco-friendly, recyclable packaging. In September, Tracey ordered fifty boxes of various types of eco-friendly packaging from Jim. She explained to Jim at the time that she was getting ready for the substantial number of orders she would usually get around Christmas time. The packaging is due to be delivered by 10· October. However, on 10th October Jim phones to say that he won't be able to deliver the packaging. Jim offers to supply Tracey with standard packaging, but Tracey refuses as she is worried about the reputational harm that would be caused to her brand if she used non-eco-friendly packaging. She cannot get eco-friendly packaging from any other source, because it is too late in the season. She has to cancel some orders that have already been placed on her website, and take most of her products off her website, meaning she misses out on Christmas orders and approximately €20,000 in profits that she would normally make at this time of year. In addition, she cannot make use of other ingredients she has purchased, worth €10,000, and she has wasted advertising costs of €2,000. Tracey wants damages to cover her lost profits and her wasted expenditure. Jim admits that Potek Packaging Ltd are in breach of contract, but refuses to pay damages to Tracey. He argues that Tracey could have mitigated her losses by accepting their offer to supply other packaging. He also argues that as most of her Christmas orders from customers had not been made yet, that she cannot definitively state that she would have made profits of €20,000, and cannot claim damages. Advise Tracey.

questions.description: In June, Marie, who runs a chain of supermarkets across Ireland, sends the… Company.
questions.text: Ross runs a transport company. In June, Marie, who runs a chain of supermarkets across Ireland, sends the following email to Ross: "We need frozen goods transported from Dublin Port to Cork - one delivery a week from 15 June to end of September. Let us know your rate and details." Ross replies: "That's great - our rate is €250 per delivery, based on a weekly delivery over that time period. Payment due monthly." Marie replies: "Fantastic - it's a deal!" However, the next week Ross decides that this was not in fact a profitable deal for him, and he would be better off entering a transport agreement with someone else. He calls Marie to cancel the agreement. Marie is furious, as she says they had a binding contract and it will now be more expensive to hire another transporter. Ross thinks he can escape the deal as Marie has not yet paid any money or any deposit. Ross also has an ongoing arrangement with a kitchen unit manufacturer, Kitchens Ltd, to transport kitchen units and kitchen equipment to different locations around the country. Whenever Kitchens Ltd needs something transported, the manager Carla simply phones Ross with the details and Ross organises the transport. This has happened approximately twice a month for the past two years, although there is no written contract in place. However, Carla has recently told Ross that she is not going to continue placing orders with him. Ross believes that an implied contract exists because of their ongoing relationship, and that Carla cannot simply cancel this contract. He believes that this implied contract includes an implied duty of good faith that requires Kitchens Ltd/Carla to give reasonable notice of six months to terminate the contract. Advise Ross.

questions.description: Tom is the manager of Mega Computers Ltd, which sells computers to both… Frustrat, Company.
questions.text: Tom is the manager of Mega Computers Ltd, which sells computers to both businesses and consumers. Tom was recently contacted by Sally, the manager of Eyre Ltd, an engineering company. Sally wanted to purchase 30 computers for the business, and asked Tom to select computers which would be suitable to run complex engineering software. Tom recommended the "Bell 2020" model, and assured Sally that this model would be suitable to run complex engineering software. Sally signs a written sales contract for the purchase of 30 Bell 2020 computers. The contract makes no mention of the ability of the computer to run the software. Unfortunately, when the employees at Eyre Ltd begin to use their computers, it transpires that the computers are very slow when running the engineering software, causing frustration amongst the employees. In addition, 3 out of the 10 computers have blurry screens and cannot be used at all. Sally is furious and wishes to return the computers and get her money back. However, when she complains to Tom, he points to Term 6 of the Terms and Conditions on the back of the signed contract which states: "All sales are final and all statutory warranties are excluded. The seller is not liable for any loss caused by any defect in any product whatsoever. No returns." Tom wants to know if Sally has a cause of action and if Term 6 will be effective to prevent any such cause of action. Advise Tom.

questions.description: Sinéad owns a hairdressing salon. Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Sinéad owns a hairdressing salon. She decides to expand her business and in early January she hires Pat to build a fully completed extension on to the salon. The contract between Sinéad and Pat clearly states that the work is to be finished by June 30th. Pat enters into a sub-contract with Charles, a local plumber in town. Charles agrees to carry out all the plumbing work on the extension by mid-May for €5,000. Pat emphasises that it is essential that this work be done on time. At the start of May Charles contacts Pat to say that the work is taking longer than he had anticipated as it is a "bigger job" than he thought. Charles says he is going to need an extra payment of €2,000 to enable him to hire extra workers so he can finish the job on time. Pat reluctantly agrees to pay the money and Charles manages to finish on time. However, once the work was done Pat writes Charles a letter demanding the return of the additional €2,000 paid, saying that it was not part of the original agreed price and that Charles has "done nothing extra for the cash". In addition, Sinéad is unhappy with the quality of some of Charles' plumbing work. She claims that the water pressure is too low for washing clients' hair and that there is a major leak from one of the sinks. She is threatening to sue Charles for breach of contract. Advise Charles on his legal position vis à vis (a) Pat [and]{.underline} (b) Sinéad.

questions.description: Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss the impact of the Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts Directive (93/13/EEC)…
questions.text: Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss the impact of the Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts Directive (93/13/EEC) on Irish contract law. In your view, has the Directive been positive for consumers? [Or]{.underline} (b) Discuss the approach of the Irish courts to a plea that a contract was entered into because of a common mistake of the contracting parties. In your view, should equitable relief be available for common mistake?

questions.description: Dervla decides to go on a trip around Ireland in a camper van. Advise Dervla.
questions.text: Dervla decides to go on a trip around Ireland in a camper van. She sees the following advertisement on a website: "Second-hand camper van for sale. All offers over €6,000 considered. If interested, please email LauraK@email.ie". On Monday Dervla sends Laura an email and the following e-mail conversation follows: Dervla: "Hi! I'm interested in the camper van but I can pay €5,000 max" Laura: "Sorry I can't sell it for less than €6,000." Dervla: "Can you hold it for me and give me a few days to think about it?" Laura: "Okay, if you really want I can hold on and not sell to anyone else for a few days. Can you get back to me by Saturday at the latest with a decision?" Dervla: "Great thank you!" On Friday, Dervla emails Laura to say she will pay €6,000 "as agreed". However, Laura tells Dervla that she already sold the camper van to another individual, Ben, that morning and so cannot sell it to Dervla. Dervla wants to now if she has entered into a binding contract for the sale of the camper van and whether he has a cause of action against Laura for selling the camper van to Ben before Saturday. Advise Dervla.

questions.description: Tech Ltd has a website on which it sells computers, tablets and other… Advise Tech Ltd.
questions.text: Tech Ltd has a website on which it sells computers, tablets and other electronic devices in Ireland. Last month an error was made by an employee when updating the website. As a result, a high-end computer which is normally sold for €5,000 was put on sale on the website for €500 for approximately 2 hours before the mistake was realised and rectified. However, in that time word had spread on social media about the "great offer" on the website, and approximately 200 customers had ordered the computer at the lower price. Each customer had received an automated email from Tech Ltd to say that the order had been received, although no money was taken from any customer's credit card or bank account at this stage. Tech Ltd does not want to sell the computers at such a low price, but angry customers have phoned the customer care line and have gone on social media to insist that they have a contractual right to the computer at the lower price. Tech Ltd asserts that no contract has been formed or alternatively, that if it has, that Tech made a very clear mistake as to the price and so should not have to sell at that low price. Advise Tech Ltd. Would your answer be different if the mistake was not on a website but rather on a price tag in a physical shop, with only one customer attempting to purchase the computer before the mistake is realised?

questions.description: Siopa Ltd is a major supermarket chain which is interested in opening a… Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Siopa Ltd is a major supermarket chain which is interested in opening a large new supermarket in a new shopping centre in Ashboume, Co. Meath. The shopping centre is owned by Centre Ltd and contains space for thirty other shops or businesses. After extensive negotiations overseen by teams of commercial solicitors, Siopa Ltd enters into a 10 year lease with Centre Ltd. Under clause 6 of the contract Centre Ltd agrees "to make best efforts to ensure that at least 90% of the centre units are filled at all times with tenants". However, after one year of the lease Siopa Ltd is unhappy with low levels of business in the supermarket. Siopa Ltd believes this is partly due to the lack of other appealing shops and businesses in the centre. The shopping centre is only 50% full, and moreover Siopa Ltd feels that some of the businesses operating in the centre are of poor quality and not able to attract customers to the shopping centre. Siopa Ltd feels that Centre Ltd has not made appropriate efforts to obtain tenants --- although it has advertised the vacant units in national newspapers, it has not actively approached potential tenants individually to invite them to lease a unit. Siopa Ltd also feels that the word "tenants" in clause 6 should be interpreted to mean "high quality" tenants, either by means of interpretation of the contract or by implication. Siopa Ltd says this is proven by Centre Ltd's own advertisements stating that "high quality tenants" are sought. Siopa Ltd is threatening to terminate the lease for breach of contract because it says Centre Ltd has not met its obligations in clause 6. However, Centre Ltd says that it has made "best efforts" to obtain tenants by advertising the vacant units, and that it could not be expected to do more. Centre Ltd also argues that the contract cannot be interpreted as meaning that "high quality' tenants must be found, either expressly or by implication. Advise Centre Ltd.

questions.description: In 2018 Pub Enterprises Ltd (PE Ltd) entered into a 10 year lease with… Consideration.
questions.text: In 2018 Pub Enterprises Ltd (PE Ltd) entered into a 10 year lease with Max, the owner of a large licensed premises. The lease stated that the premises was only to be used as a pub/bar. However, in March 2020, owing to Government restrictions during the Covid pandemic, the pub was forced to close and PE Ltd struggled to pay the agreed rent. Max agreed to accept a reduced rent from PE Ltd, "for as long as the Government restrictions due to Covid continue". There was no consideration for the promise to accept a lower rent, nor was the promise formalised in a deed under seal. By August 2021 the restrictions were partially lifted, but PE Ltd's business was still operating at only 50% of its usual capacity. However, Max is claiming that the full rent rate should resume, and moreover is now claiming payment of the unpaid portion of the original rate of rent for the period from March 2020 to July 2021. Advise PE Ltd.

questions.description: Travel Ltd is a travel agent that specialises in cruises around Europe,… Advise Travel Ltd.
questions.text: Travel Ltd is a travel agent that specialises in cruises around Europe, and exclusively sells cruise tickets on behalf of European Cruises Ltd (EC Ltd). The contract between Travel Ltd and EC Ltd states that EC Ltd will allocate 200 tickets to Travel Ltd each month, and pay commission for tickets sold. The contract can be terminated by EC Ltd at one month's notice. This arrangement ran successfully for five years. However, a dispute recently arose whereby Travel Ltd alleged that EC Ltd had failed to pay the correct amount of commission. EC Ltd denied these claims, and gave notice that it would terminate the arrangement between them by giving one month's notice. This would have put Travel Ltd out of business. EC Ltd then offered to enter into a new contract with Travel Ltd. This new contract included a new term by which Travel Ltd waived any claims it might have for any previously unpaid commission. When Travel Ltd's manager was shown this contract he expressed doubts about this new term, but EC Ltd refused his request to take a copy of the contract with him in order to discuss it and obtain legal advice. Travel Ltd entered into the new contract. Travel Ltd now wants to bring a claim against EC Ltd for the unpaid commission. It wants to know whether the new contract it entered into can be set aside on the basis that it was entered into under economic duress. Advise Travel Ltd.

questions.description: Transport Shipping Ltd hired a ship to Grains Ltd in June for a hire fee… Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Transport Shipping Ltd hired a ship to Grains Ltd in June for a hire fee of €2,000 per day. The ship was due to be returned on 31st August and so Transport Shipping Ltd arranged to hire the same ship out to a third party, McCann Ltd, for 6 months from 1st September. Transport Shipping Ltd was able to negotiate a hire fee of €4,000 per day for this second hire period with McCann Ltd. This was a particularly high fee which Transport Shipping Ltd was able to negotiate as its ship had unique characteristics which McCann Ltd required. Grains Ltd failed to return the ship until 30th September. Grains Ltd admits it is in breach of contract and is willing to pay damages to cover an extra month's hire at the contract rate of €2,000 per day (a total of €60,000). As a result of the late delivery of the ship, however, Transport Shipping Ltd was forced to renegotiate its contract with McCann Ltd to allow for a delayed start to the hire period and Transport Shipping Ltd suffered losses of €200,000 as a result. Grains Ltd says that it should not be liable for these losses as it was unaware of the particularly high fee which Transport Ltd had negotiated. However, Grains Ltd does admit that it was aware that it was likely that other contracts would be impacted by the delayed return of the ship. Transport Shipping Ltd wants to know what damages it might be able to claim under general contract law. Advise Transport Shipping Ltd.

questions.description: Lucy runs a public relations business called Publicity Ltd. Advise Lucy.
questions.text: Lucy runs a public relations business called Publicity Ltd. Just over three months ago Lucy entered into a 2-year written contract with a small business, Instant Fame Ltd (IF Ltd), under which IF Ltd agreed to manage the social media aspects of Lucy's business. A fee is payable monthly. During pre-contract discussions, Claude, the owner of IF Ltd, told Lucy that he had "successfully managed social media accounts for several Irish celebrities, including Sile Shardashion". However, three months into the contract, Lucy meets Sile Shardashion. Sile says that while Claude did manage her social media account it was only for a few weeks and she had to fire him as he made a number of serious errors, as a result of which she lost followers on several social media platforms. Lucy is furious at Claude for misleading her like this. She wishes to cancel her contract with Claude and seeks your advice. In addition, in June Lucy entered into a I-year written contract with Max to design and maintain her business website. Performance was due to begin on September 1st, with payment due every month. However, in July Lucy decided that Max was a bit expensive, and she wanted to cancel the contract with him and hire someone else instead. She believes that as Max hasn't commenced work and she hasn't paid a deposit or other payment, that she should be able to cancel this contract. Advise Lucy.

questions.description: Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss how exemption clauses are controlled by legislation in Ireland.
questions.text: Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) (a) Discuss how exemption clauses are controlled by legislation in Ireland. What impact has the European Union had on the regulation of exemption clauses and unfair terms? [Or]{.underline} (b) Discuss the approach of the Irish courts to penalty clauses in contracts.
